---
title: "Double Trouble" # October Post
subtitle: "Doubly Robust Methods For Unmeasured Confounding"
author: "Ryan Batten"
date: "2026-03-25"
#image: dr_unmeasured.png
categories: [Entropy Balancing, IP Weighting]
bibliography: ebal_ipw.bib
draft: true
format: 
  html:
    code-fold: true
---

# Paralysis by Analysis: Causal Methods

Causal inference using observational data can be quite useful, however we need to account for confounding. Unfortunately, we can't use randomization however there is a litany of methods to choose from. Propensity score matching (note: a variety of matching methods are available), inverse probability weighting, outcome regression, targeted machine learning estimation, augmented inverse probability weighting, doubly robust methods....the list goes on and on.

A common question becomes how do we choose between methods? This post will look to compare between two methods:

-   Entropy Balancing

-   Inverse Probability Weighting

# Weight a Minute

Before we dive into these methods, we need to law some groundwork for what each are, and how they work.

## Inverse Probability Weighting

Inverse probability weighting is a useful tool for causal inference.

## Entropy Balancing

Entropy balancing works by balancing entropy between the two groups. What is entropy? Glad you asked!

In statistics, entropy refers to: \[insert what entropy is\]

The fact entropy balancing uses equations is a double edged sword. On the one hand

# Start the Comparing!

Alright, so if we're going to compare methods, there are a few ways we can do this. A fantastic resource is [@morris2019using]. For this post, we are going to compare the two methods, specifically for:

1.  Balance

2.  Bias

3.  Precision

We can define each of these as follows

Alright, now we are going to enter the simulation!

# Balance

For valid causal inference, one of the assumptions is exchangeability. Essentially, to do this we need to use a method that will account for this. With real-world data, we don't have that. Instead, we have methods that we can use. Here we are going to inspect balance a few ways:

1.  Visual
2.  Using standardized mean differences
3.  

# Bias

# Precision 

# Enter the Simulation

To see whether this hair-brained scheme could work, we'll simulate some data. Let's lay out some ground rules for our simulation:

-   Going to run it 1000 times (chosen due to run time/don't want our laptops to implode)

-   Sample size of 250 (arbitrary)

-   Two measured confounders (one continuous, one binary)

-   Two unmeasured confounders (both continuous)

-   Binary treatment

-   Continuous outcome

-   Target estimand will be the average treatment effect (ATE)

Methods we'll explore:

-   Individual probability weighting only

-   Generalized linear model (outcome model only)

-   Doubly robust: using the same covariates in both the propensity score model and the outcome model

```{r setup, message=FALSE, warning = FALSE}

# Title: Setup 

# Description: This code is the setup for what we will be using later. It includes the libraries and any functions that we may need. 

library(tidyverse) # ol' faithful
library(WeightIt) # for IP weighting
library(kableExtra) # for formatting table output
library(ggdag) # for drawing a DAG 

#... Functions ----

# We will use this function to simulate data 

sim_data <- function(n = 250, # sample size 
                     beta_trt = 1.5, # treatment effect
                     # Parameters for Z1 
                     z1_mean = 5, z1_sd = 1, 
                     # Parameters for Z2
                     z2_size = 1, z2_prob = 0.5, 
                     # Parameters for U1
                     u1_mean = 10, u1_sd = 3,
                     u2_mean = 2, u2_sd = 0.5,
                     # Confounder - Effect on X
                     z1_on_x = 0.05, z2_on_x = 0.5, 
                     u1_on_x = 0.08, u2_on_x = 0.8, 
                     # Confounder - Effect on Y
                     z1_on_y = 0.5, z2_on_y = 0.7,
                     u1_on_y = 0.4, u2_on_y = 2
){
  
  # Creating the Dataframe
  
  df <- data.frame(
    z1 = rnorm(n = n, mean = z1_mean, sd = z1_sd), # measured continuous confounder
    z2 = rbinom(n = n, size = z2_size, prob = z2_prob)# measured binary confounder
  ) %>% 
    dplyr::mutate(
      beta_trt = 1.5, 
      prob = plogis(u1_on_x*u1 - u2_on_x*u2 + z1_on_x*z1 + z2_on_x*z2), 
      x = rbinom(n = n, size = 1, prob = prob), 
      y = beta_trt*x + z1_on_y*z1 + z2_on_y*z2 + u1_on_y*u1 + u2_on_y*u2 + rnorm(n = n, mean = 0, sd = 1)
      #y = beta_trt*x + z1_on_y*z1 + z2_on_y*z2 + rnorm(n = n, mean = 0, sd = 1)
    )
  
  # Return the dataframe
  
  return(df)
}

```

And of course, we need a directed acyclic graph (DAG)!

```{r}

theme_set(theme_dag())

dag_example <- ggdag::dagify(
  x ~ z1 + z2 + u1 + u2, 
  y ~ z1 + z2 + u1 + u2 + x, 
  exposure = "x",
  outcome = "y"
)

ggdag::ggdag(dag_example, layout = "nicely")

```

## Perfectly Imperfect

For our first scenario, let's assume that we perfectly specify both models based on the data we have. We will include both of the measured confounders (z1 & z2), in each model. However, the model will still be imperfect since there is unmeasured confounding.

How does it look for each method?

```{r perfect_world, cache = TRUE, warning = FALSE}

# Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW) ----


```
